24: yevarechecha YHWH veyishmerecha yaer YHWH panav eleicha vichuneka yissa YHWH panav eleicha veyasem lecha shalom: The Aaronic Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) is the oldest liturgical text in continuous use — fragments of it were found on silver amulets from the 7th century BCE (the Ketef Hinnom scrolls, the oldest biblical text discovered). Its three-part structure increases in length: 3 words, 5 words, 7 words, with a crescendo toward shalom (peace/wholeness/well-being). The divine name YHWH appears three times — early Christian interpreters saw this as a Trinitarian hint. The blessing has been recited in Jewish synagogues for over 2,500 years and by Christian ministers at service benedictions, making it one of the most-spoken texts in human history.
1: YHWH's word comes to Moses with a new category of voluntary consecration — the Nazirite law. The formal address pattern (waydabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor) signals legislation rather than narrative, placing the Nazirite institution on par with priestly and sacrificial law.
2: נָזִיר (nāzîr) derives from the root נזר (nzr), meaning to separate, dedicate, or consecrate oneself. The cognate noun נֵזֶר (nēzer) means crown or consecration-mark (see v7), showing that Nazirite status carries a visible dignitary dimension. The vow is open to both men and women (îš ô ʾiššâ), unusually egalitarian in ancient law, creating a temporary lay priestly status.
3: The first prohibition is total abstention from grape products, including שֵׁכָר (šēkār) — strong drink, likely barley beer — alongside יַיִן (yayin), wine. The MKT renders this distinction as wine/beer. The prohibition extends to wine vinegar and beer vinegar: even fermented derivatives are banned. The comprehensiveness signals that the issue is not intoxication alone but total separation from the vine as a category.
4: The exhaustive list continues: fresh grapes, dried grapes (raisins), grape seeds (ḥarṣan), and grape skin/husks (zāg). The specificity ensures no technical loophole. This total-abstinence-from-the-vine pattern echoes the Rechabites (Jer 35) and anticipates the eschatological vine abstinence Jesus declares before the Last Supper (Mark 14:25). The Nazirite's vine-separation embodies a particular holiness-orientation: belonging entirely to YHWH rather than to the ordinary pleasures of Canaan.
5: The second prohibition: no razor may touch the Nazirite's head during the vow period. The uncut hair is explicitly called נֵזֶר (nēzer) — the same word for the golden diadem worn by the high priest (Exod 29:6; Lev 8:9) and for the royal crown. The Nazirite's flowing hair is a visible crown of consecration, marking dedicated status the way priestly vestments mark ordination. He is qādôš — holy to YHWH — throughout the vow period.
6: The third prohibition: no contact with a נֶפֶשׁ מֵת (nep̱eš mēt), a dead person. The phrase uses nep̱eš (soul/life) rather than gûp̄ (body), emphasizing the entire personal reality of the deceased. This prohibition matches the high priest's restriction (Lev 21:11) — not merely the ordinary priestly restriction of avoiding all but close family (Lev 21:1-3). The Nazirite assumes, for the vow period, the purity standard of Israel's highest office.
7: Even parents, siblings, or children — the close family for whom ordinary priests may defile themselves (Lev 21:2-3) — are off-limits to the Nazirite. The reason given: kî nezer ʾĕlōhāyw ʿal-rōʾšô — because the crown of his God is upon his head. The nēzer (hair-crown) embodies the divine claim on the Nazirite; severing this relationship, even for grief, would violate the consecration. Personal love must yield to higher consecration — a pattern that appears again in Jesus's teaching about discipleship and family ties (Matt 10:37).
8: The Nazirite is declared qādôš — holy — for the entire period of separation. Holiness in Hebrew thought is not primarily a moral predicate but a relational one: belonging to YHWH, set apart from ordinary use. The Nazirite's holiness is temporary but total, encompassing diet, hair, and purity — a comprehensive bodily consecration.
9: Accidental defilement — someone dying suddenly in his presence — voids the entire accumulated period. The language veṭimmēʾ rōʾš nizrô — he defiles his consecrated head — shows the hair (crown) as the locus of consecration. Defilement requires shaving the head on the seventh day (the standard purification completion day) and presenting it for burning, eliminating the consecration-symbol entirely.
10: On the eighth day — the day of new beginnings across sacrificial law (Lev 8:33; 9:1; 14:10,23; 15:14,29) — the Nazirite brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons. This is the minimum-cost offering (used for the poor in Lev 5:7; 12:8), suggesting the provision includes those of limited means.
11: One bird serves as חַטָּאת (ḥaṭṭāʾt) — sin offering — and one as עֹלָה (ʿōlâ) — burnt offering. The priest makes atonement for the Nazirite, with the explicit purpose stated: ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ ʿal-hannāp̱eš — because he sinned by reason of the dead person. The accidental defilement, though involuntary, requires atonement — a key principle of the sacrificial system: moral intent and ritual state are both real categories requiring different responses.
12: After atonement, the Nazirite reconsecrates his days to YHWH and brings a אָשָׁם (ʾāšām) — guilt/reparation offering — of a year-old male lamb. The previous days of the vow are yipplû — they fall, are forfeited. The ʾāšām compensates for the breach of sacred obligation; it is the offering for unauthorized use of holy things (Lev 5:14-6:7). The void-and-restart structure means the Nazirite's consecration period cannot be partially valid — it is all-or-nothing.
13: The completion ceremony begins when the full vow term is satisfied. The Nazirite is brought to the tent of meeting — the ceremony is formal and communal. zōʾt tôrat hannāzîr — this is the law/instruction of the Nazirite — frames what follows as comprehensive legislation for the concluding rites.
14: Three animals form the core of the completion offering: (1) a male lamb without blemish (תָּמִים, tāmîm) for the burnt offering, (2) a female lamb without blemish for the sin offering, (3) a ram without blemish for the fellowship offering. The use of tāmîm — the same term used for the Passover lamb (Exod 12:5) and throughout Levitical sacrificial law — signals that the Nazirite's completion offering meets the highest standard of unblemishedness.
15: The grain and drink accompaniments follow the fellowship offering: a basket of unleavened bread (loaves and wafers), grain offering, and drink offerings. The full complement of offerings — burnt, sin, fellowship, grain, drink — represents the complete sacrificial repertoire, making the Nazirite's completion a comprehensive act of worship.
16: The priest officiates the sin and burnt offerings on the Nazirite's behalf. The sin offering precedes the burnt offering — standard sequence in Levitical ritual: atonement enables ascent (the burnt offering = total dedication ascending to God).
17: The ram as fellowship (שֶׁלֶם, šelem) offering — the communal meal offering shared between YHWH, priest, and worshiper — forms the climax of the completion ceremony. The basket of unleavened bread accompanies it. The fellowship offering celebrates the restored or completed relationship; completing a vow is precisely the occasion it serves (Lev 7:16).
18: The Nazirite's consecrated hair (rōʾš nizrô — the head of his consecration) is shaved at the tent of meeting entrance. The hair is placed under the fellowship offering pot and burned. This act consummates the vow: the nēzer (consecration-mark) is offered to YHWH in the fire. The Nazirite's separation is ended not by mere completion of time but by a ritual act that transfers the embodied consecration to YHWH.
19: The priest performs the wave offering with three items: the boiled shoulder of the ram, an unleavened loaf, and a wafer. The shoulder — a priestly portion under fellowship offerings (Lev 7:32-33) — is here waved along with bread before being given to the priest. This distinguishes the completion ceremony from a standard fellowship offering by the additional ritual action.
20: After the wave offering, it becomes qōdeš (holy portion) for the priest, alongside the wave breast and contribution thigh. Following this, the Nazirite may drink wine. The explicit permission signals that the wine-abstinence was the visible marker of the vow, and lifting it marks the definitive return to ordinary status. The entire sequence — shaving, offering, priestly portion, permission to drink — is a formal ceremony of return.
21: The concluding rubric summarizes: this is the law of the Nazirite who makes a vow. It adds a qualifying principle: the required minimum can be exceeded — whatever else his resources allow. Vow law generally holds that voluntary pledges can go beyond the minimum; the law establishes a floor, not a ceiling. The phrase al-pi nidrô — according to his vow — makes the entire law dependent on the sincere fulfillment of whatever was specifically pledged.
22: The Aaronic blessing (vv22-27) begins with a second revelation formula, signaling a distinct legal unit appended to the Nazirite legislation. The two laws share thematic connection: the Nazirite law ends with the priest conferring sacred portions; immediately following, the priests receive the formula by which YHWH himself blesses through their speech.
23: Aaron and his sons are commanded to bless the Israelites with this specific formula. The verb בָּרַךְ (bārak) in the Piel = actively confer blessing; this is performative speech, not a wish. The priests are the agents, but v27 clarifies that YHWH is the effective cause. The formula uses three couplets with increasing length, each beginning with the divine name YHWH and containing 3, 5, and 7 words respectively in Hebrew — a carefully structured oral text.
25: yāʾēr YHWH pānāyw ʾēleykā wîḥunneka — May YHWH make his face shine on you and be gracious to you. פָּנִים (pānîm) — face — is the Hebrew idiom for personal presence, character, and favor. A shining face (ʾôr pānîm) signifies delight and approval (Ps 31:16; 80:3,7,19; 119:135). חנן (ḥānan) — be gracious — describes sovereign, unearned favor; חֵן (ḥēn) is the relational grace underlying covenant relationship. The face-shining idiom appears in Moses's prayer for divine presence after the golden calf (Exod 33:18-23), where seeing the face is the fullest possible intimacy with God.
26: yiśśāʾ YHWH pānāyw ʾēleykā weyāśēm ləkā šālôm — May YHWH lift up his face toward you and grant you peace. נָשָׂא פָּנִים (nāśāʾ pānîm) — to lift the face toward someone — means to give full favorable attention, the opposite of turning away or hiding the face (Ps 102:2; 143:7). שָׁלוֹם (šālôm) — peace — encompasses far more than absence of conflict: completeness, wholeness, flourishing of every relationship. It is the summary term for the totality of covenant blessing (Num 25:12; Isa 54:10; Ezek 34:25).
27: weśāmû ʾet-šəmî ʿal-bənê yiśrāʾēl waʾănî ʾăbārekēm — They shall put my name on the Israelites, and I will bless them. The priests' recitation of the three-fold formula puts the divine name on the people — blessing is the act of naming, of declaring whose they are. YHWH appears three times in vv24-26, once per couplet; the three-fold invocation of the Name is the structural engine of the blessing. The final clause shifts to first person singular — I will bless them — YHWH claiming direct agency over what the priests pronounce. Priestly mediation and divine immediacy are not in tension; the priest's words are the vehicle of YHWH's own action.
